tldr: Migrating from New Relic to Datadog is a full observability re-platform: dashboards, alerts, APM services, browser/RUM, synthetics and logs all need to be re-modeled and the agent fleet re-rolled. Plan for a multi-week dual-run window, and budget carefully — Datadog's per-host + per-product SKUs typically come in higher than New Relic's GB-based ingest model.
why_switch: Deeper out-of-the-box integration breadth (Datadog publishes 700+ integrations across cloud, container, queue and DB stacks; New Relic ships fewer first-party integrations).
Tighter unified product surface — APM, infra, logs, RUM, Synthetics, CSM, Cloud SIEM and DBM all share one tag model and one navigation, which can reduce context-switching versus New Relic One.

Stronger agent ergonomics: a single Datadog Agent autodiscovers containers and emits metrics, traces, logs and process data with one config tree, versus juggling the New Relic Infra agent + APM agents + Pixie/eBPF stack.
More mature host-tag based correlation — Datadog's tag everywhere model (env/service/version + custom tags) is often easier to standardize than NR entity GUIDs once you cross 500+ hosts.
Wider community of dashboard/monitor templates and a public Marketplace for third-party packs.
Better-developed downstream surfaces (Workflow Automation, Incident Management, Cloud SIEM, DBM) for teams that want to consolidate vendors beyond pure APM.
If the team is already heavy on Kubernetes + AWS, Datadog's cluster agent and Cloud Cost Management hooks tend to be lower-friction than NR's equivalents.
what_to_export: Custom dashboards (pages, widgets, NRQL queries) — pulled via NerdGraph dashboard entities
Alert policies and NRQL alert conditions (thresholds, durations, runbooks)
Workflows, destinations and notification channels (Slack, PagerDuty, email)
APM application list with language, framework and entity GUID — needed to map to Datadog services
Browser monitoring apps and any custom Browser instrumentation
Synthetic monitors (ping, simple browser, scripted browser, API tests) including scripts and locations
Log parsing rules, drop filters, data partitions and saved log views
Custom metrics and custom events (Metric and Event types in NRDB)
Service levels (SLI/SLO) definitions if you use New Relic SLM
Infrastructure agent config, integrations YAML, and any custom on-host integrations
Tag taxonomy / entity tags applied across hosts, services and apps
Users, teams, roles and SSO configuration (mapped to Datadog teams/roles, not auto-importable)
export_method:
Most New Relic configuration data is reachable through NerdGraph, the GraphQL API at api.newrelic.com/graphql. Per the official docs NerdGraph supports querying dashboards (the actor.entity(guid) query returns a full dashboard with pages, widgets and NRQL queries), running NRQL across one or more accounts, and pulling distributed tracing data. Dashboards can also be exported as files (PDF/PNG) directly from the UI, but for migration you want the structured JSON-equivalent that NerdGraph returns so widgets can be transformed. There are dashboardCreate / dashboardUpdate / dashboardDelete mutations for round-tripping if you need to script bulk operations.

For APM, browser and synthetics, treat NerdGraph's entity model as the inventory: query entities by domain (APM, BROWSER, SYNTH, INFRA) and by entity type, capture each entity's GUID, name, language/runtime and tags, then export attached configuration (synthetic scripts via the synthetics mutation surface, alert conditions via the alerts queries). NRQL queries themselves are pure text and can be archived to a repo as you walk dashboards and conditions; that text is what later gets rewritten into Datadog query syntax.

Telemetry data already in NRDB is a separate problem. NerdGraph is explicitly not a data ingest API and there is no first-party bulk dump of historical metrics/logs/traces in a Datadog-importable format. The pragmatic pattern is: keep New Relic running read-only for the existing retention window (the official policy varies by data type and contract), and only replay live data forward into Datadog. If long-term retention is required, use New Relic's Streaming Data Export or scheduled NRQL pulls into S3 and treat that as cold archive, not as something Datadog will index.
import_method:
Datadog dashboards are JSON documents with a stable shape (title, layout_type, widgets[], template_variables[]). The supported workflow is to POST that JSON to the dashboards API (api.datadoghq.com/api/v1/dashboard, or v2 for newer surfaces) using a Datadog API key + application key, or to manage them as code with the official Datadog Terraform provider (datadog_dashboard_json or the typed datadog_dashboard resource). For monitors, the equivalent path is the monitors API or the datadog_monitor Terraform resource. Synthetics tests, SLOs, logs pipelines and notebooks all have parallel API + Terraform resources, so Terraform is usually the cleanest landing pad for a migration of this size.

The agent rollout is the load-bearing step. The Datadog Agent is installed per host (or as a DaemonSet on Kubernetes via the datadog-agent Helm chart / Operator) and per the official getting-started docs it ships default checks for CPU, disk, IO, memory, network, NTP and uptime, plus container/process/log/trace pipelines when enabled. Install methods include the one-line script, OS packages, Chef/Puppet/Ansible, the Helm chart, the Datadog Operator, and Fleet Automation for centralized lifecycle management. APM is added by enabling the tracer library for each language (dd-trace-*) alongside the agent; logs are enabled by setting logs_enabled: true and pointing log sources at the agent.

The practical sequence is: (1) stand up the Datadog org and Terraform repo, (2) roll the Datadog Agent + tracers next to the existing New Relic agents in dual-ship mode for a representative slice, (3) re-create the most critical dashboards and monitors as Terraform, (4) burn down the long tail of dashboards/alerts category by category, (5) cut over alert routing once monitor noise is acceptable, (6) remove New Relic agents host-by-host. Expect cardinality and cost surprises in step 3-4; it is normal to rewrite dashboards rather than translate widget-for-widget.

concept_mapping.datadog: Datadog query language — metrics use 'avg:trace.servlet.request.duration{env:prod}', logs use the Logs Search syntax, traces use APM Analytics; no 1:1 SQL-style equivalent, queries must be rewritten
APM Service (identified by service tag, with env and version as the unified service tagging trio)
RUM Application (Datadog RUM SDK, separate SKU from APM)

concept_mapping.new_relic: Synthetics monitor (ping / simple / scripted browser / API)
concept_mapping.datadog: Synthetic Test (HTTP / Browser / API / Multistep API), with public + private locations
Datadog Agent + integration checks (conf.d/<integration>.yaml) plus Autodiscovery for containers
Logs (indexed) + Log Pipelines / Processors; archived logs go to Log Archives, rehydrated on demand
Custom Metric (with cardinality budget) or Custom Event via the events API
Tags (host tags, metric tags, log/trace tags) — unify on env, service, version first
Dashboards (timeboards / screenboards merged into one) — JSON shape with widgets[] + template_variables[]

effort: 3-10 weeks for a mid-size estate (dozens of services, a few hundred hosts). Small fleets with a handful of dashboards and Kubernetes-only infra can finish in 2-3 weeks; large enterprises with hundreds of dashboards, scripted synthetics, custom NRQL alerts and on-prem hosts should plan a full quarter and a phased per-team rollout.
gotchas: NRQL does not translate cleanly to Datadog query language — every dashboard widget and every alert condition needs to be rewritten by hand or with an LLM-assisted pass, and the semantics of FACET, TIMESERIES and percentile functions differ from Datadog's group-by + rollup model.

Running both agents on the same host doubles CPU/memory overhead and can double-count metrics if both scrape the same /metrics endpoint or the same kube-state-metrics; isolate the dual-run window per host, not forever.

Datadog custom metric cardinality is the single biggest cost trap. New Relic bills on GB ingested, Datadog bills per-custom-metric (with a generous-but-finite included pool). Audit tag cardinality before you ship a dashboard that fans out by user_id or request_id.

Log indexing economics differ: New Relic charges on log GB ingested, Datadog charges on indexed events with separate retention SKUs. You almost always want exclusion filters and Log Archives in front of indexing, or the bill changes shape unpredictably.

APM service naming: New Relic's app names rarely match the env/service/version tagging Datadog expects. Decide your service taxonomy before installing tracers — renaming services later breaks dashboards, monitors and SLOs that pin to the service tag.

Alert noise during cutover is real. New monitors fire on first evaluation against unfamiliar baselines; stagger monitor activation, start with @-handles muted or routed to a low-priority channel, and only promote to PagerDuty after a clean week.

Browser/RUM is not a drop-in: New Relic Browser uses one snippet, Datadog RUM uses a separate SDK with its own session sampling and privacy config. Plan a dedicated week for RUM cutover and re-validate funnels.

Historical telemetry does not migrate. Year-over-year comparisons, postmortem links and saved NRQL queries point to data only in NRDB. Keep a read-only New Relic seat for the contracted retention period instead of trying to backfill.

email.subject: Plan: moving observability from New Relic to Datadog
email.body:
Hi team,

Writing up the plan for the New Relic to Datadog move so we are aligned before the agent rollout starts.

Scope. We are re-platforming observability end-to-end: APM, infra, logs, browser/RUM, synthetics, dashboards and alerts. Historical NRDB data does not migrate — we keep a read-only New Relic seat for the remaining retention window, and only replay live telemetry forward into Datadog.

Approach. Export configuration from New Relic via NerdGraph (dashboards, entities, alert conditions, synthetic scripts), land it in a Terraform repo, then re-create dashboards and monitors as code in Datadog using the official provider. The Datadog Agent and language tracers get rolled host-by-host (Helm chart on Kubernetes, package install on VMs) in dual-ship mode alongside the existing New Relic agents until each surface is verified.

Sequence. (1) Stand up the Datadog org and the Terraform repo. (2) Agree on the env/service/version tagging convention before any tracer ships — this is load-bearing. (3) Dual-ship one representative service end-to-end and validate APM, logs, infra, dashboard and alert parity. (4) Burn down the long tail of dashboards and monitors category by category. (5) Cut alert routing once monitor noise is acceptable. (6) Remove New Relic agents host by host.

Risks I want us to track. NRQL queries do not translate one-for-one to Datadog query language and will be rewritten manually. Datadog bills per-custom-metric and per-indexed-log-event, so cardinality and indexing filters need an explicit owner — this is where the bill drifts. Alert noise during cutover is the other big risk; we will stage monitors muted before promoting them to PagerDuty.

Timeline. Realistic estimate is 3-10 weeks depending on how many services and dashboards we keep versus retire. I will follow up with a per-team rollout schedule once we agree on the service taxonomy.

Happy to walk through any part of this live.

Thanks
